section: ABSTRACT
text: If the fissionable and fertile materials recoverable at approximately today's cost are to constitute an energy reserve at least as large as fossil-fuel energy reserves, about $20\%$ of the fertile material has to be burned.

section: ABSTRACT
text: This means reactors need a conversion ratio of about $96\%$ in the average.

section: ABSTRACT
text: Some reactors will of necessity be burners, hence the $96\%$ average can only be obtained if other reactors are breeders.

section: ABSTRACT
text: Comparison of nuclear-fission energy reserves with anticipated power demands indicates that breeding will not be necessary until 1980.

section: ABSTRACT
text: Whether it will become a necessity between 1980 and 2000 depends on which of a number of reasonable estimates are chosen.

section: ABSTRACT
text: The latter doubling time is estimated to be 5 to 10 years.

section: THE NEED FOR U²³³ BREEDING
text: The Oak Ridge National Laboratory has conducted a study regarding breeding on the $\mathbf{Th}^{232}\text{-}\mathbf{U}^{233}$ cycle.

section: THE NEED FOR U²³³ BREEDING
text: Object of the study was, on one hand, the importance of breeding on this cycle and, on the other, a comparison of the various reactor types with respect to their suitability as $\mathbf{U}^{233}$ breeders.

section: THE NEED FOR U²³³ BREEDING
text: The importance of breeding on the $\mathbf{Th}^{232}\text{-}\mathbf{U}^{233}$ cycle depends, in turn, on the importance of breeding in general, and secondly on the comparison of the $\mathbf{U}^{238}\text{-}\mathbf{Pu}^{239}$ breeding cycle with the $\mathbf{Th}^{232}\text{-}\mathbf{U}^{233}$ cycle.

section: I. THE NECESSITY FOR BREEDING - GENERAL REMARKS
text: The fuel burnup cost in a straight burner, with present prices, is about 3 mills/kwh.

section: I. THE NECESSITY FOR BREEDING - GENERAL REMARKS
text: Thus a difference of $10\%$ in conversion ratio amounts to about 0.3 mills/kwh, since a reactor of conversion ratio $\beta$ could buy fuel amounting to $10\%$ of its burnup for 0.3 mills/kwh and end up with the same amount of fissionable material as a reactor of breeding ratio $\beta + 0.1$ .

section: I. THE NECESSITY FOR BREEDING - GENERAL REMARKS
text: A breeder and a converter of reasonably high conversion ratio will not differ in conversion ratio by more than a small multiple of $10\%$ , and the difference in fuel-burnup cost will thus be smaller than the uncertainty in the estimated power cost of a nuclear reactor.

section: I. THE NECESSITY FOR BREEDING - GENERAL REMARKS
text: The long-range planning is needed in the nuclear-power field because of the long development and design time - estimated at 15 years - and long life of power plants, estimated at 25 years.

section: I. THE NECESSITY FOR BREEDING - GENERAL REMARKS
text: Thus, if breeding will be necessary $15 + 25 = 40$ years hence, that is about by the year 2000, it is not too early to proceed with the development now.

section: I. THE NECESSITY FOR BREEDING - GENERAL REMARKS
text: Otherwise, we will have, 40 years hence, a large installed capacity which still could be used except for the fact that it burns fissionable material which we can no longer afford to burn.

section: I. THE NECESSITY FOR BREEDING - GENERAL REMARKS
text: If there is a reasonable probability of breeding being attractive during the next 40 years, such development would be indicated.

section: II. THE NECCESSITY OF BREEDING - QUANTITATIVE CONSIDERATIONS
text: The known US uranium reserves recoverable at approximately the present cost of $10/lb U308 are 230,000 tons, to which 350,000 tons should be added on the basis of specific geological evidence in the areas known to contain deposits.

section: II. THE NECCESSITY OF BREEDING - QUANTITATIVE CONSIDERATIONS
text: This gives a total of 580,000 tons.

section: II. THE NECCESSITY OF BREEDING - QUANTITATIVE CONSIDERATIONS
text: Known reserves in the United States recoverable at $30 to $50/lb of U308 are 6,000,000 tons.* The energy content of 1 ton of U308, if all the uranium is used, amounts to 5.9 x 1013 Btu.

section: II. THE NECCESSITY OF BREEDING - QUANTITATIVE CONSIDERATIONS
text: If only the U235 is burned, the energy content of 1 ton of U308 would be 3.6 x 1011 Btu.** Thus, 580,000 tons of reasonably certain US reserves of high-grade ore would amount to 34 x 1018 Btu, or 0.21 x 1018 Btu, depending on whether all of the uranium, or only the U235, is burned.

section: II. THE NECCESSITY OF BREEDING - QUANTITATIVE CONSIDERATIONS
text: For comparison, the US reserves of oil and gas recoverable at up to 1.3 times the present cost, plus the US reserves of other fossil fuels recoverable at up to twice the present cost, amount to 6.9 x 1018 Btu.

section: II. THE NECCESSITY OF BREEDING - QUANTITATIVE CONSIDERATIONS
text: Thus, at least 20% of the U238 has to be burned before the uranium recoverable at approximately today's prices contributes as much to the energy reserves of the US as do the fossil fuels recoverable at the above cost.

section: II. THE NECCESSITY OF BREEDING - QUANTITATIVE CONSIDERATIONS
text: This 20% burnup corresponds to a 96.4% conversion ratio.

section: II. THE NECCESSITY OF BREEDING - QUANTITATIVE CONSIDERATIONS
text: Hence, the nuclear energy reserves will be greater than the fossil reserves only if conversion ratios in excess of $96.4\%$ are obtained.

section: II. THE NECCESSITY OF BREEDING - QUANTITATIVE CONSIDERATIONS
text: Breeding requires conversion ratios of $100\%$ or more.

section: II. THE NECCESSITY OF BREEDING - QUANTITATIVE CONSIDERATIONS
text: Though it becomes the more difficult to increase the conversion ratio by $1\%$ the higher the conversion ratio already is, it does not appear that breeding would be much harder to achieve than a conversion ratio of $96.4\%$ .

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: From a practical viewpoint, the main difference between plutonium and $U^{233}$ breeding lies in the inventory of fissionable material and in the theoretically achievable breeding gain.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: The inventory is larger for plutonium breeders than for $U^{233}$ breeders.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: This is mainly a consequence of basic physical facts: because of the energy dependence of the $\eta$ of $\mathsf{Pu}^{239}$ , plutonium breeders have to operate at high neutron energies where the cross sections are small and where it takes many plutonium atoms to catch a neutron with sufficient probability before it escapes or slows down.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: The U $^{233}$ breeders, on the other hand, operate best in the thermal region where the cross sections are large, and fewer atoms suffice to prevent an adequate number of neutrons from escaping.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: Thus, the critical mass and inventory in a U $^{233}$ breeder can be made very low, and the specific power very high.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: The design parameters of a typical thermal breeder (a 300 Mwe aqueous homogeneous reactor station) call for about $4500\mathrm{kw}$ (thermal) per kilogram of fissionable material.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: With this specific power a breeding gain of $10\%$ would correspond to 4.2 years doubling time.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: The Enrico Fermi fast breeder reactor has a critical mass of $485\mathrm{kg}$ for 300 MW (thermal) output, that is 600 kw (thermal) per kilogram of fissionable material.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: In the first assemblies the holdup of fissionable material in the blanket and the external reprocessing cycle might be as much as three additional critical masses, which would reduce the specific power to 150 kw (thermal) per kilogram of fissionable material.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: If the first core achieves a net breeding gain of $10\%$ (taking into account chemical reprocessing losses) the doubling time would be 100 years, that is, it would be irrelevant if compared to the doubling time of the electrical power production.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: On the other hand, future fast breeders, in particular those fueled with plutonium, may have higher breeder gains, by a factor of 5, as indicated above.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: Another factor of 2 or so may be obtained by cutting down on the holdup in the external reprocessing cycle, increasing the power density and so on.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: This would give doubling times of about 10 years for the fast breeders.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: The doubling time of the nuclear power production between 1980 and 2000 is 5 to 10 years,

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: Another important point of comparison for the breeding cycles is the availability of the fertile materials, uranium for the plutonium cycle, and thorium for the $U^{233}$ cycle.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: There is more thorium than uranium in the earth's crust, but there is more uranium than thorium in ores recoverable at today's prices.<sup>1</sup> At some price for the oxide, the availability of thorium must equal that of uranium but it is not known whether this price will be anywhere near a price at which nuclear energy is practical.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: The North American continent, U.S. and Canada, have about $200,000^{1}$ tons of high-grade thorium ore, which is a fraction of the high-grade uranium ore supply but still on the same order of magnitude and very substantial.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: If all converted into energy this supply would correspond to $13 \times 10^{18}$ Stu which is quite comparable to the whole fossil fuel supply of the U.S. and Canada.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: It would cover the anticipated U.S. requirement of electrical energy well beyond the year 2000.

section: III. COMPARISON OF PLUTONIUM AND U233 BREEDING
text: A strong case can be made for parallel development of the plutonium and $U^{233}$ breeding cycles.
